Arguments.prompt: Download the US state boundary shapefile from the US Census Bureau TIGER/GENZ2022 dataset at this URL: https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2022/shp/cb_2022_us_state_20m.zip. Also download the US county boundary shapefile from: https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2022/shp/cb_2022_us_county_20m.zip. From the state boundary file, filter to the single feature representing Oregon (state FIPS code '41'). From the county boundary file, filter to only those features whose STATEFP attribute equals '41', retaining all Oregon counties. Reproject both the Oregon state boundary layer and the Oregon county boundary layer to the EPSG:5070 coordinate reference system (Conus Albers Equal Area). From the reprojected Oregon state boundary geometry, compute the bounding box in EPSG:4326 (WGS84 geographic coordinates) by temporarily reprojecting the state boundary to EPSG:4326 and extracting the total_bounds (minx, miny, maxx, maxy). Save the Oregon state boundary as a GeoJSON file named 'oregon_state.geojson', the Oregon county boundaries as a GeoJSON file named 'oregon_counties.geojson', and the bounding box as a plain JSON file named 'oregon_bbox.json' containing keys 'minx', 'miny', 'maxx', and 'maxy' representing the WGS84 bounding box. All three output files must be written to the local staging directory so they are automatically persisted to S3 by the FaaSr system.

This action processes MODIS satellite imagery to produce monthly NDVI composites for Oregon and a statewide mean NDVI time series. It consumes three files produced by the previous step: a GeoJSON file containing the Oregon state boundary reprojected to EPSG:5070, a GeoJSON file containing Oregon county boundaries reprojected to EPSG:5070, and a JSON file containing the Oregon bounding box in EPSG:4326 with keys 'minx', 'miny', 'maxx', and 'maxy'. Discover these files from the registry at runtime.

Use the bounding box values to construct a spatial filter for querying the Microsoft Planetary Computer STAC API. Query the STAC API at https://planetarycomputer.microsoft.com/api/stac/v1 for the collection named 'modis-13A1-061' (MODIS 16-day NDVI composite at 500-meter resolution). Perform this query month by month for all 12 calendar months of the year 2023 (January 2023 through December 2023). For each month, issue a STAC search with the Oregon bounding box as the spatial filter and the month's date range as the temporal filter (e.g., '2023-01-01/2023-01-31' for January). If a month returns zero items, skip it silently and record it as a skipped month. If a month raises any exception during processing, log the error message, record it as a skipped month, and continue to the next month without halting.

For each month that returns valid results, process the imagery as follows: sign each asset URL using the planetary_computer.sign() function before downloading; download the asset named '500m_16_days_NDVI' for each scene using rioxarray (rxr.open_rasterio with masked=True); reproject each individual tile to EPSG:5070 using the reproject method; to mosaic all reprojected tiles for the month, write each reprojected tile to an in-memory rasterio MemoryFile (or a temporary file) and then use rasterio.merge.merge on those open dataset objects to produce a single merged raster covering Oregon; clip the mosaicked raster to the Oregon state boundary polygon loaded from the GeoJSON file; convert raw integer pixel values to floating-point NDVI by dividing by 10000.0; mask out any pixels whose resulting value falls outside the range -1.0 to 1.0 (set them to NaN); if multiple scenes from different acquisition dates fall within the same calendar month, compute a pixel-wise nanmean across all valid pixels to produce a single monthly composite array.

Only months that were successfully processed (not skipped) are included in the outputs. Skipped months are omitted entirely from both the GeoTIFF and the metadata — do not insert placeholder or NaN bands for skipped months. The number of bands in the output GeoTIFF must exactly match the number of entries in the output metadata JSON list.

After processing all months, compute the mean NDVI value across all spatially valid (non-NaN) Oregon pixels for each successfully processed month, producing a list of scalar mean NDVI values in the same order as the processed months.

Save the monthly composite rasters as a single multi-band GeoTIFF file named 'ndvi_monthly_composites.tif' where each band corresponds to exactly one successfully processed month (skipped months are excluded), preserving the CRS and transform metadata. Save the time series data as a CSV file named 'ndvi_timeseries.csv' with columns 'month_label' (abbreviated month and year string, e.g. 'Jan 2023') and 'mean_ndvi' (the scalar mean NDVI value for that month), containing only rows for successfully processed months. Also save a JSON metadata file named 'ndvi_metadata.json' containing a list of month label strings (e.g. ['Jan 2023', 'Feb 2023', ...]) in the same order as the bands in the GeoTIFF, containing only successfully processed months — the length of this list must equal the number of bands in the GeoTIFF. Write all output files to the local staging directory so they are automatically persisted to S3 by the FaaSr system.

This action produces a monthly NDVI map grid figure for Oregon. It consumes the following files produced by upstream steps: a multi-band GeoTIFF file containing monthly NDVI composites for Oregon (one band per successfully processed month, values in floating-point NDVI units, CRS EPSG:5070), a JSON metadata file containing a list of month label strings (e.g. 'Jan 2023') in the same order as the GeoTIFF bands, a GeoJSON file containing the Oregon state boundary in EPSG:5070, and a GeoJSON file containing Oregon county boundaries in EPSG:5070. Discover all input files from the registry at runtime.

Load the GeoTIFF using rioxarray (rxr.open_rasterio), load the metadata JSON to retrieve the ordered list of month labels, and load both boundary GeoJSON files using geopandas. The number of bands in the GeoTIFF exactly matches the number of entries in the metadata JSON list — use this list to label each band correctly.

Create a matplotlib figure with a 16-by-9 inch aspect ratio arranged as a fixed grid of 3 rows and 4 columns (12 panel slots total). Always use this 3x4 layout regardless of how many months of data are available. For each panel slot from 0 to 11: if a corresponding band exists (i.e., the band index is within the number of available bands), display the NDVI composite for that band using the rioxarray DataArray's plot method on a matplotlib Axes object (use da.plot(ax=ax, cmap='YlGn', vmin=-1, vmax=1, add_colorbar=False) or equivalent), which correctly handles geospatial extents in the native EPSG:5070 coordinate space. After plotting the raster on the axes, overlay the Oregon state boundary using geopandas GeoDataFrame.plot(ax=ax, facecolor='none', edgecolor='black', linewidth=1.0) and overlay the Oregon county boundaries using GeoDataFrame.plot(ax=ax, facecolor='none', edgecolor='darkgray', linewidth=0.5). Both boundary GeoDataFrames are already in EPSG:5070 and will align correctly with the raster. Label each panel with its corresponding month label string as the panel title using a small font size (e.g., fontsize=8). Remove all axis ticks and spines from every panel. If a panel slot has no corresponding data band (i.e., fewer than 12 months were processed), turn off that panel's axes entirely (ax.set_visible(False)).

After creating all panels, add a single shared colorbar for all data panels. Create the colorbar using a ScalarMappable with the YlGn colormap and vmin=-1, vmax=1, and label it 'NDVI'. Position the colorbar below the grid or to the right of the grid.

Save the figure as a PNG file named 'ndvi_monthly_map_grid.png' at 200 dots per inch. Write the output file to the local staging directory so it is automatically persisted to S3 by the FaaSr system.

This action produces an annotated mean NDVI time series line plot for Oregon. It consumes a CSV file containing the statewide mean NDVI time series produced by an upstream step. The CSV has columns 'month_label' (abbreviated month and year string, e.g. 'Jan 2023') and 'mean_ndvi' (scalar mean NDVI value), containing only rows for successfully processed months. Discover the input file from the registry at runtime.

Load the CSV using pandas. Create a matplotlib figure at least 10 inches wide and an appropriate height (e.g., 6 inches). Plot a line chart with the month labels on the horizontal axis (x-axis) and the mean NDVI values on the vertical axis (y-axis). Use markers at each data point (e.g., marker='o'). Enable a light background grid with alpha around 0.4. Label the horizontal axis 'Month' and the vertical axis 'Mean NDVI'. Add a descriptive title such as 'Statewide Mean NDVI Over Time — Oregon'. Rotate horizontal axis tick labels by 45 degrees for readability and apply tight layout.

Before attempting to detect interior local extrema, check whether the series has at least 3 data points. If the series has fewer than 3 data points, skip the extrema detection and annotation step entirely without raising an error. If the series has 3 or more data points, detect all interior local maxima and minima — that is, indices i where 1 <= i <= len(series)-2 such that the value is strictly greater than both neighbors (local maximum) or strictly less than both neighbors (local minimum). For each detected interior peak or trough, add a text annotation on the plot showing the month label and the NDVI value rounded to three decimal places (e.g., 'Jan 2023\n0.412'). Position annotations slightly above peaks (positive vertical offset) and slightly below troughs (negative vertical offset) to avoid overlapping the line. Use a small font size for annotations (e.g., fontsize=8).

Save the figure as a PNG file named 'ndvi_timeseries_plot.png' at 150 dots per inch with tight bounding box layout (bbox_inches='tight'). Write the output file to the local staging directory so it is automatically persisted to S3 by the FaaSr system.
